prompt:
Gym software again. The route-setting board UI needs a query object over the in-memory route sheet (an array of hashes with symbol keys — :name, :grade, :wall, :setter, :days_up, whatever the sheet has that week), and I want it done the ActiveRecord-relation way, not the way the last contractor did it (filtered copies everywhere, everything computed eagerly, scopes that mutated their parent — we chased that bug for a month).

Build `RouteQuery` in `route_query.rb`. `RouteQuery.new(rows)` wraps the sheet. Then:

- `where(hash)` — equality on each key, multiple keys AND together, and chained wheres AND together. A row that doesn't even have the key never matches. `where { |row| ... }` — block form for anything fancier. Passing BOTH a hash and a block raises ArgumentError "where takes a filter hash or a block, not both"; passing neither raises ArgumentError "where needs a filter hash or a block".
- `order(:key)` ascending by default, `order(:key, :desc)` descending; anything else for the direction is ArgumentError "direction must be :asc or :desc, got :sideways" (whatever they passed, inspect form). Chained orders compose — first call is the primary sort, later calls break ties. And ordering must be STABLE: rows that compare equal stay in the order they had in the sheet, at ANY sheet size. Ruby's sort_by does not promise that, so it's on you.
- Terminals: `to_a` (a fresh array each call, containing the actual row hashes from the sheet, not copies), `count`, `pluck(:key)` (flat array of values), `pluck(:a, :b)` (array of [a, b] arrays).

Two hard requirements, because this is where the old one hurt us:

1. LAZY. Building a chain does zero work — no filter runs, no sort happens, until a terminal is called. Terminals re-evaluate every time they're called (no result caching), and they see the sheet as it is AT THAT MOMENT — routes appended to the source array after the chain was built but before the terminal show up in the results.
2. IMMUTABLE LINKS. Every where/order returns a NEW RouteQuery; the receiver is untouched. Two different refinements built off the same base must not contaminate each other or the base. And nothing you do may mutate the source array or its hashes — the whole thing has to work on frozen rows.

`test_route_query.rb` in this directory is the acceptance contract — exact messages, tie orders, laziness probes, all of it. Don't touch the test file. Done when `ruby test_route_query.rb` is green.
